elements.sent-2411-001: This paper presents a novel approach for nonlinear assimilation called score-based sequential Langevin sampling (SSLS) within a recursive Bayesian framework.
elements.sent-2411-002: SSLS decomposes the assimilation process into a sequence of prediction and update steps, utilizing dynamic models for prediction and observation data for updating via score-based Langevin Monte Carlo.
elements.sent-2411-003: An annealing strategy is incorporated to enhance convergence and facilitate multi-modal sampling.
elements.sent-2411-004: The convergence of SSLS in TV-distance is analyzed under certain conditions, providing insights into error behavior related to hyper-parameters.
elements.sent-2411-005: Numerical examples demonstrate its outstanding performance in high-dimensional and nonlinear scenarios, as well as in situations with sparse or partial measurements.
elements.sent-2411-006: Furthermore, SSLS effectively quantifies the uncertainty associated with the estimated states, highlighting its potential for error calibration.
elements.sent-2411-007: This task is essential in various application scenarios such as weather forecasting~\citep{Katsafados2020Numerical,Evensen2022Data , digital twin technology~\citep{Thelen2022comprehensive1,Thelen2022comprehensive2 , and mathematical finance~\citep{Bhar2010Stochastic,Rudiger2012Pricing,Elliott2013option .}}}
elements.sent-2411-008: Despite its importance and widespread application, data assimilation remains a challenging task.
elements.sent-2411-009: The major difficulties in data assimilation lie in the nonlinear nature of both the state transition dynamics and the measurement model, as well as the high dimensionality of the state.
elements.sent-2411-010: Moreover, in many cases, only noisy and sparse observation data are available, introducing further difficulties to the data assimilation.
elements.sent-2411-011: Apart from estimating the latent states, researchers also need to quantify the uncertainties of the estimated states, which is crucial for assessing and calibrating the estimation error~\citep{Sullivan2015Introduction .}
elements.sent-2411-012: These constraints and requirements pose significant challenges for data assimilation.
elements.sent-2411-013: The variational methods, such as 3D-Var and 4D-Var~\citep{LeDimet1986Variational , estimate the latent states through maximum-a-posteriori.}
elements.sent-2411-014: In contrast, Bayesian filtering, including the ensemble Kalman filter (EnKF)~\citep{houtekamer1998data and the particle filter (PF)~\citep{Gordon1993Novel,Kitagawa1996Monte,Doucet2001Sequential , aims to sample from the posterior distribution.}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Despite their widespread use, these methods face challenges when dealing with complex assimilation problems.
elements.sent-2411-016: Specifically, the rationale behind variational methods and EnKF invokes a Gaussian approximation to the posterior distribution~\citep{Sarkka20232023Bayesian .}
elements.sent-2411-017: To this end, they assume a Gaussian prior and a Gaussian measurement noise, as well as linearize the dynamics and measurement models.
elements.sent-2411-018: However, in the highly nonlinear assimilation scenarios with non-Gaussian prior and likelihood, the posterior may be far away from a Gaussian distribution~\citep{Mandel2012convergence , leading to poor performance of the Gaussian approximation.}
elements.sent-2411-019: On the other hand, the PF suffers from a particle degeneracy or impoverishment in high-dimensional assimilations~\citep{Snyder2008Obstacles,Bengtsson2008Curse,Bickel2008Sharp , although the PF does not rely on the linear and Gaussian assumptions.}
elements.sent-2411-020: This refers to the phenomenon that when the number of ensemble particles is restricted, with high probability many of the particles in the ensemble will turn out to have the same value~\citep{Snyder2008Obstacles .}
elements.sent-2411-021: This mode collapse arises due to the limited number of particles utilized in the PF, which are inadequate to fully capture the intricate structure of the high-dimensional posterior distribution.
elements.sent-2411-022: Their exceptional performance in high-dimensional problems has sparked a surge of interest in utilizing diffusion models for data assimilation~\citep{Rozet2023score,li2024state,Bao2024score,si2024latent .}
elements.sent-2411-023: However, a key challenge arises when estimating the time-dependent likelihood~\citep{chung2023diffusion , as existing methods rely on either linear Gaussian assumptions or biased approximations.}

elements.sent-2411-025: In contrast,~\citet{Bao2024score,si2024latent approximate the time-dependent likelihood by scaling the time-independent likelihood with a time-dependent damping function.}

elements.sent-2411-030: Our main contributions are summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-031: We present a novel method for nonlinear assimilation, named score-based sequential Langevin sampling (SSLS), within a recursive Bayesian framework.
elements.sent-2411-032: SSLS decomposes the assimilation process into a sequence of iterations invoking prediction and update steps.
elements.sent-2411-033: During the prediction step, we utilize the dynamics model to predict states, from which the score of the prior distribution can be learned.
elements.sent-2411-034: Subsequently, in the update step, we incorporate the observation data as the likelihood and sample from the posterior distribution using the score-based Langevin Monte Carlo.
elements.sent-2411-035: To improve convergence and facilitate multi-modal sampling, an annealing strategy is integrated into the Langevin algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-036: We analyze the convergence of SSLS in TV-distance under certain mild conditions.
elements.sent-2411-037: Our theoretical findings characterize precisely how the assimilation error is affected by the various hyper-parameters including the terminal time, the score estimation error, the discretization error, and the initial distribution shift (Theorem~\ref{theorem:section:convergence:assimilation ).}
elements.sent-2411-038: In addition, our theoretical results provide valuable insights into the convergence of score-based Langevin-type algorithms for posterior sampling (Theorem~\ref{theorem:section:convergence ), which is of independent interest.}
elements.sent-2411-039: We utilize SSLS in various numerical examples to assess its performance and compare it with baseline approaches from multiple perspectives.
elements.sent-2411-040: According to our experimental results, SSLS yields significant advantages in high-dimensional and nonlinear data assimilation, even with only sparse observations.
elements.sent-2411-041: Furthermore, the standard deviation of SSLS accurately indicates estimation errors, highlighting the proficiency of our method in quantifying uncertainty.

elements.subsection-2411-006: Update via annealed Langevin Monte Carlo
elements.sent-2411-081: This subsection introduces an annealed Langevin Monte Carlo (ALMC) to sample from the posterior distribution $\pi_{k+1 (\cdot|\vy_{[k+1] )$ in~\eqref{eq:bayes .}}}
elements.sent-2411-082: The Langevin diffusion associated with the target distribution $\pi_{k+1 (\cdot|\vy_{[k+1] )$ is defined as the solution to the stochastic differential equation \begin{equation}\label{eq:method:update:LD} \d\mZ_{t}=\nabla_{\vx}\log\pi_{k+1}(\mZ_{t}|\vy_{[k+1]})\dt+\sqrt{2}\d\mB_{t}, \quad \mZ_{0}\sim \what{q}_{k+1}(\cdot|\vy_{[k]}),\end{equation} where $(\mB_{t )_{t\geq 0}$ is a Brownian motion.}}}
elements.sent-2411-083: Classically, if $\pi_{k+1}$ satisfies a functional inequality such as Poincare's inequality or log-Sobolev inequality, then the law of Langevin diffusion~\eqref{eq:method:update:LD converges exponentially fast to the target distribution $\pi_{k+1}$~\citep{Bakr2014Analysis .}}
elements.sent-2411-084: However, in most of the cases, Langevin diffusion~\eqref{eq:method:update:LD can not be simulated analytically, and the exact score function is intractable.}
elements.sent-2411-085: The Euler-Maruyama discretization~\citep{Kloeden1992Numerical of the Langevin diffusion with the estimated score~\eqref{eq:dsm leads to the score-based LMC, which defines a discrete-time non-homogeneous Markov chain \begin{equation}\label{eq:method:update:LMC} \what{\mZ}_{(\ell+1)h}=\what{\mZ}_{\ell h}+h\what{\vb}_{k+1}(\what{\mZ}_{\ell h}|\vy_{[k+1]})+\sqrt{2h}\vxi_{\ell}, \quad \what{\mZ}_{0}\sim\what{q}_{k+1}(\cdot|\vy_{[k]}),\end{equation} where $h>0$ is the step size, $(\vxi_{\ell )_{\ell\in\bbN}$ is a sequence of independent standard Gaussian variables, and the drift term is the sum of the log-likelihood and the estimated prediction score function~\eqref{eq:dsm \begin{equation}\label{eq:method:update:posterior:score} \what{\vb}_{k+1}(\mZ_{t},\vy_{[k+1]})=\nabla_{\vx}\log g_{k+1}(\vy_{k+1}|\vx)+\what{\vs}_{k+1}(\vx|\vy_{[k]}).\end{equation}.}}}}

Initialize the particles $\what{\mZ}_{0}^{1,i}\leftarrow\mX_{k+1}^{i}$ for each $1\leq i\leq n$. \For {$m=1,\ldots,M$}{ \For {$\ell=0,\ldots,K-1$}{ Sample independent Gaussian noises $\vxi_{\ell}^{m,1},\ldots,\vxi_{\ell}^{m,n}\sim^{\iid}N(0,\mI)$. Compute the estimated posterior score $\what{\vb}_{k+1}^{m}(\cdot|\vy_{[k+1]})\leftarrow\beta_{m}\nabla_{\vx}\log g_{k+1}(\vy_{k+1}|\cdot)+\what{\vs}_{k+1}(\cdot|\vy_{[k]})$. Update by the LMC, for $1\leq i\leq n$, $\what{\mZ}_{(\ell+1)h}^{m,i}\leftarrow\what{\mZ}_{\ell h}^{m,i}+h\what{\vb}_{k+1}^{m}(\what{\mZ}_{\ell h}^{m,i}|\vy_{[k+1]})+\sqrt{2h}\vxi_{\ell}^{m,i}$.} Initialize the particles for the next round $\what{\mZ}_{0}^{m+1,i}\leftarrow\what{\mZ}_{Kh}^{m,i}$ for $1\leq i\leq n$.} $\what{\mX}_{k+1}^{i}\leftarrow\what{\mZ}_{Kh}^{M,i}$ for $1\leq i\leq n$.

elements.sent-2411-261: The outcomes indicate remarkable performance of SSLS in tasks such as super-resolution, sparse reconstruction, and box reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-262: In the super-resolution task, as shown in the second row of Figure~\ref{fig:KolmogorovEvolution , the observation data loses nearly all of the micro-structures of the reference vorticity field.}
elements.sent-2411-263: However, the states estimated by SSLS are able to reconstruct a majority of the intricate details.
elements.sent-2411-264: In the task of sparse reconstruction, SSLS exhibits remarkable performance even when masking out a substantial 88.72\% of all points, as illustrated in the fourth and fifth rows of Figure~\ref{fig:KolmogorovEvolution .}
elements.sent-2411-265: This underscores the robustness of SSLS in handling observation sparsity, which is crucial in application scenarios where observation costs are high and the number of measurements is limited.
elements.sent-2411-266: It is worth noting that in the challenging task of box reconstruction, the observations within the masked box are completely obscured.
elements.sent-2411-267: However, SSLS exhibits impressive performance even within the masked domain, as depicted in the last row of Figure~\ref{fig:KolmogorovEvolution .}
elements.sent-2411-268: In the aforementioned tasks, there is a notable discrepancy between the estimated states and the observation data, suggesting that the prior information have a substantial impact on the assimilation.
elements.sent-2411-269: The efficiency of the prior in the assimilation will be investigated further through an ablation study in subsequent experiments.
elements.sent-2411-270: However, mere point estimation is insufficient, particularly in situations where the reliability of the estimations is of utmost importance.

elements.sent-2411-272: To this end, we consider the random reconstruction task as an example, where the observation data are obtained by masking 95\% of grid points randomly and perturbating with Gaussian noise.

elements.subsection-2411-015: Diffusion models for Bayesian inverse problems
elements.sent-2411-302: In recent years, diffusion methods have gained prominence as an effective technique for posterior sampling in Bayesian inverse problems, such as~\citet{chung2023diffusion,song2023pseudo,purohit2024posterior .}
elements.sent-2411-303: This section provides a comprehensive review of the existing literature on diffusion-based approaches for posterior sampling.
elements.sent-2411-304: Suppose the forward model is defined as \begin{equation}\mU=\calG(\mZ,\mW), \quad \mZ\sim p_{\mZ},\end{equation} where $\calG$ represents a given measurement model, $p_{\mZ}$ is a known prior distribution, and $\mW$ is a random variable with a known distribution.
elements.sent-2411-305: As a result, the measurement likelihood $p_{\mU|\mZ (\vu|\vz)$ can be obtained from the measurement model $\calG$ and the distribution of $\mW$.}
elements.sent-2411-306: It is evident that the update step (Section~\ref{section:method:update ) within the recursive Bayesian framework exactly corresponds to solving a Bayesian inverse problem.}
elements.sent-2411-307: The mainstream technique in diffusion models for conditional or posterior sampling is guidance~\citep{guo2024gradient .}
elements.sent-2411-308: As an illustrative example, we consider the diffusion model with the following forward process \begin{equation}\d\mZ_{s}=-\mZ_{s}\ds+\sqrt{2}\d\mB_{s}, \quad \mZ_{0}\sim p_{\mZ},~ s\in(0,T),\end{equation} where $(\mB_{s )_{s\geq 0}$ is a standard Brownian motion.}
elements.sent-2411-309: Denote by $p_{\mZ_{s }$ the law of $\mZ_{s}$ for each $s\in(0,T)$.}
elements.sent-2411-310: Following Bayes' rule, the time-reversal process for sampling from the posterior distribution $p_{\mZ|\mU (\cdot|\vu)$ reads~\citep{chung2023diffusion \begin{equation}\label{eq:related:diffusion:reversal} \begin{aligned} \d\bar{\mZ}_{s} =\big\{\bar{\mZ}_{s}-2\overbrace{\nabla_{\vz}\log p_{\mU|\mZ_{T-s}}(\vu|\bar{\mZ}_{s})}^{\text{intractable}}-2\overbrace{\nabla_{\vz}\log p_{\mZ_{T-s}}(\bar{\mZ}_{s})}^{\text{score}}\big\}\ds+\sqrt{2}\d\mB_{s}, \bar{\mZ}_{0} \sim N(0,1), ~ s\in(0,T), \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $p_{\mU|\mZ_{s (\vu|\vz)$ is the time-dependent likelihood, and $p_{\mZ_{s (\vz)$ is the time-dependent prior.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-311: It worth noting that the score in~\eqref{eq:related:diffusion:reversal can be estimated by denoising score matching~\citep{Vincent2011Connection,song2021score , while the gradient of log-likelihood is typically intractable.}}
elements.sent-2411-312: When the measurement model $\calG$ is linear, and both the prior and the likelihood are Gaussian, that is, \begin{equation}\calG(\mZ,\mW):=\mG\mZ+\sigma_{\obs}\mW, \quad \mZ\sim p_{\mZ}=N(\mu,\mathbf{\Sigma}),~\mW\sim N(0,\mI_{d}),\end{equation} the gradient of time-dependent log-likelihood in~\eqref{eq:related:diffusion:reversal can be estimated without bias.}
elements.sent-2411-313: For a detailed derivation, please refer to~\citet[Lemma 1]{guo2024gradient .}
elements.sent-2411-314: However, this linear scenario corresponds to the assimilation with linear state-space model.
elements.sent-2411-315: The solution to the linear assimilation can be obtained using the ensemble Kalman filter~\citep{Sarkka20232023Bayesian .}
elements.sent-2411-316: Therefore, there is no necessity to utilize diffusion models for the linear assimilation.. \parNonlinear inverse problems.
elements.sent-2411-317: Researchers' interests lie in nonlinear inverse problems, which corresponds to the nonlinear assimilation.
elements.sent-2411-318: One of the most widely-used diffusion-based approaches for nonlinear inverse problem is the diffusion posterior sampling (DPS)~\citep{chung2023diffusion , which estimates the gradient of time-dependent log-likelihood in~\eqref{eq:related:diffusion:reversal by exchanging the expectation with likelihood, that is, \begin{equation}p_{\mU|\mZ_{s}}(\vu|\vz) =\int p_{\mU|\mZ_{0}}(\vu|\vz_{0})p_{\mZ_{0}|\mZ_{s}}(\vz_{0}|\vz)\d\vz_{0} \nonumber =\bbE\big[p_{\mU|\mZ_{0}}(\vu|\mZ_{0})|\mZ_{s}=\vz\big]\approx p_{\mU|\mZ_{0}}(\vu|\bbE[\mZ_{0}|\mZ_{s}=\vz]). \label{eq:related:diffusion:DPS}\end{equation} However, it is important to note that this approximation introduces a bias term known as Jensen's gap, as pointed out by~\citet{chung2023diffusion .}}}
elements.sent-2411-319: Therefore, DPS can not yield a consistent estimation in nonlinear Bayesian inverse problems.
elements.sent-2411-320: In contrast, the score-based LMC proposed by this work is consistent in nonlinear scenarios, as shown in Theorem~\ref{theorem:section:convergence .}
elements.sent-2411-321: Building upon these methods, a variety of diffusion-based approaches have been developed for data assimilation, which can be broadly classified into data-driven methods and filtering methods.
elements.sent-2411-322: A comparison of these techniques, along with commonly used classical methods, is presented in Table~\ref{table:comparison .}
elements.figure-2411-012: \caption{A comparison of methods for nonlinear assimilation.} \begin{tabular}{lcl} Method Consistent Approximation or assumption 3D-Var/4D-Var \ding{56} ~(i) Linearization (ii) Gaussian prior and likelihood SDA~\citep{Rozet2023score} \ding{56} Jensen's gap~\citep{chung2023diffusion} SOAD~\citep{li2024state} \ding{52} Gaussian prior and likelihood EnKF \ding{56} ~(i) Linearization (ii) Gaussian prior and likelihood PF \ding{52} None SF~\citep{Bao2024score} \ding{56} Damping function SSLS (ours) \ding{52} None \end{tabular} \label{table:comparison}
elements.sent-2411-323: Data-driven methods focus on estimating the posterior distribution of all latent states $\mZ:=\mX_{[k]}$ given all available observation data $\mU:=\mY_{[k]}$.
elements.sent-2411-324: By substituting $\mZ$ and $\mU$ into the time-reversal process~\eqref{eq:related:diffusion:reversal , this line of methods reformulate the assimialtion as a single Bayesian inverse problem~\citep{Rozet2023score,rozet2023scoreqg,li2024state .}}
elements.sent-2411-325: This characteristic makes these methods data-driven, as they rely on empirical data rather than explicit knowledge of the underlying physical models, i.e., the states transition dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-326: In these data-driven approaches, the physical mechanism is implicitly incorporated into the prior score.
elements.sent-2411-327: For example, the score-based data assimilation (SDA)~\citep{Rozet2023score,rozet2023scoreqg estimates the gradient of time-dependent log-likelihood in a similar manner as DPS~\eqref{eq:related:diffusion:DPS .}}
elements.sent-2411-328: In~\citet{li2024state , the authors propose the state-observation augmented diffusion (SOAD) method, which involves converting a nonlinear state-space model into a linear one through variable augmentation.}
elements.sent-2411-329: The gradient of time-dependent log-likelihood of augmented linear state-space model can be estimated without bias, as discussed in the previous section.
elements.sent-2411-330: This limitation of the data-driven methods hinders the application of these methods to high-dimensional data assimilation tasks.
elements.sent-2411-331: Additionally, data-driven methods are unable to fully leverage the underlying physical mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-332: In contrast, the dimensionality of SSLS proposed in this study remains constant, regardless of the assimilation time.
elements.sent-2411-333: Moreover, SSLS effectively integrates physical principles with observation data.
elements.sent-2411-334: Another category of diffusion-based assimilation methods are developed within the Bayesian filtering framework (Section~\ref{section:method:Bayes ), with examples including the score-based filter (SF)\citep{Bao2024score .}}
elements.sent-2411-335: In the update at the $(k+1)$-th time step, SF sets $\mU:=\mY_{k+1}$, $\vu:=\vy_{k+1}$, and $\mZ_{0}\sim\what{q _{k+1 (\cdot|\vy{[k] )$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-336: SF estimates the prior score in\eqref{eq:related:diffusion:reversal using sliced score matching~\citep{song2020Sliced , while approximating the gradient of the time-dependent log-likelihood through a separation of variables: \begin{equation}\nabla_{\vz}\log p_{\mU|\mZ_{s}}(\vu|\vz)=h(s)\nabla_{\vz}\log p_{\mU|\mZ_{0}}(\vu|\vz), \quad s\in(0,T),\end{equation} Here, the damping function $h$ is a monotonically decreasing function in the interval $[0,T]$, with $h(0)=1$ and $h(T)=0$.}}
elements.sent-2411-337: However, this separation of variable approximation is often inconsistent, and the optimal choice of the damping function remains unresolved.
elements.sent-2411-338: In contrast, our method is consistent and does not rely on such heuristic approximations.
elements.sent-2411-339: The convergence of SSLS in TV-distance is examined under mild conditions, offering insights into error behavior related to hyper-parameters.
elements.sent-2411-340: Numerical examples showcase the exceptional performance of SSLS in high-dimensional and nonlinear scenarios, as well as situations with sparse or partial measurements.
elements.sent-2411-341: Additionally, SSLS effectively quantifies the uncertainty of estimated states, highlighting its potential for error calibration.
elements.sent-2411-342: While our current work assumes knowledge of the state-space model, there may be unknown parameters in the dynamics or measurement models.
elements.sent-2411-343: Future research will focus on extending SSLS to simultaneously estimate latent states and unknown parameters within the state-space model.
elements.sent-2411-344: Additionally, the computational cost of training a score network in each time step can be significant.
elements.sent-2411-345: Our future work aims to reduce this cost through techniques such as in-context learning.
elements.sent-2411-346: Furthermore, we are interested in expanding our method to assimilation with time sparsity.. \bibliographystyle{plainnat .}

elements.sent-2411-536: This paper presents score-based sequential Langevin sampling (SSLS), a novel approach to nonlinear data assimilation within a recursive Bayesian framework.
elements.sent-2411-537: The proposed method decomposes the assimilation process into alternating prediction and update steps, leveraging dynamic models for state prediction while incorporating observational data through score-based Langevin Monte Carlo during updates.
elements.sent-2411-538: To address challenges in posterior sampling, we introduce an annealing strategy within the update mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-539: We provide theoretical guarantees for SSLS convergence in total variation (TV) distance under certain conditions, providing insights into error behavior with respect to key hyper-parameters.
elements.sent-2411-540: Our numerical experiments across challenging scenarios -- including high-dimensional systems, strong nonlinearity, and sparse observations -- demonstrate the robust performance of the proposed method.
elements.sent-2411-541: Furthermore, SSLS effectively quantifies the uncertainty associated with the estimated states, making it particularly valuable for the error calibration.
elements.sent-2411-542: These approaches generally fall into two categories: variational methods~\citep{Evensen2022Data and Bayesian filtering~\citep{Sarkka20232023Bayesian .}}
elements.sent-2411-543: Variational methods, such as 3D-Var and 4D-Var~\citep{LeDimet1986Variational , estimate latent states through maximum-a-posteriori inference.}
elements.sent-2411-544: In contrast, Bayesian filtering approaches, including the ensemble Kalman filter (EnKF)~\citep{houtekamer1998data and particle filter (PF)~\citep{Gordon1993Novel,Kitagawa1996Monte,Doucet2001Sequential , aim to sample from the posterior distribution.}}
elements.sent-2411-545: Despite their widespread adoption, both categories encounter significant limitations in complex assimilation scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-546: The fundamental rationale behind variational methods and EnKF relies on Gaussian approximations of the posterior distribution~\citep{Sarkka20232023Bayesian .}
elements.sent-2411-547: These methods assume both Gaussian prior and measurement noise, while linearizing the dynamics and measurement models.
elements.sent-2411-548: However, in highly nonlinear assimilation scenarios with non-Gaussian prior and likelihood, the true posterior may deviate substantially from a Gaussian distribution~\citep{Mandel2012convergence , severely compromising the effectiveness of Gaussian approximations.}
elements.sent-2411-549: The PF, while free from linear and Gaussian assumptions, encounters particle degeneracy or impoverishment in high-dimensional settings~\citep{Snyder2008Obstacles,Bengtsson2008Curse,Bickel2008Sharp .}
elements.sent-2411-550: This phenomenon occurs when the number of ensemble particles is limited: with high probability, multiple particles in the ensemble converge to identical values~\citep{Snyder2008Obstacles .}
elements.sent-2411-551: While these approaches demonstrate encouraging empirical performance in certain nonlinear and high-dimensional problems, they face two key limitations: they either depend on restrictive assumptions about Gaussian priors and likelihoods~\citep{li2024state , or they lack rigorous theoretical foundations~\citep{Rozet2023score,Bao2024score,si2024latent .}}
elements.sent-2411-552: A detailed discussion of these limitations is provided in Appendix~\ref{section:related .}
elements.sent-2411-553: Our theoretical findings characterize precisely how the assimilation error is affected by the various hyper-parameters including the step size, the number of iterations, the score matching error, and the initial distribution shift (Theorem~\ref{theorem:section:convergence:assimilation ).}

elements.sent-2411-558: This preference arises because posterior sampling provides direct access to statistical inference through the computation of essential measures such as means, standard deviations, and confidence intervals, which are crucial for decision-making and uncertainty quantification.

elements.sent-2411-676: However, APF exhibits a notable delay following state mutations.
elements.sent-2411-677: This delay arises because APF approximates the prediction distribution using a weighted particle set.
elements.sent-2411-678: Due to state mutations, particles sampled from the prediction distribution may be far from the observation, resulting in small assigned weights.
elements.sent-2411-679: These particles thus contribute minimally to the posterior distribution approximation~\citep[Chapter 11.6]{Sarkka20232023Bayesian .}
elements.sent-2411-680: This phenomenon, known as particle degeneracy, persists even though APF offers some improvement over the standard PF.
elements.sent-2411-681: SSLS employs a score network, while EnKF uses a Gaussian distribution to approximate the prediction distribution.
elements.sent-2411-682: Both methods can generalize to regions near the observation where predicted particles may be absent, enabling them to respond more rapidly to state mutations.

elements.sent-2411-684: This advantage arises because EnKF only captures linear components while disregarding higher-order structures of the dynamics model.

elements.sent-2411-686: In contrast, SSLS and APF maintain their performance despite the nonlinear measurement model.

elements.sent-2411-689: Our objective is to track a velocity field describing the solutions to the NS equation~\eqref{P3:kolmogorov with unknown initial condition.}
elements.sent-2411-690: The SSLS assimilation process begins with a set of independent random realizations of $\vu_{0}$.
elements.sent-2411-691: A crucial consideration is that the initial reference state $\vu_{T_{0 }$ follows a distribution that differs from the initial distribution employed in SSLS.}
elements.sent-2411-692: Such initial distributional shifts are prevalent in practical applications, where the true distribution of the initial state often remains unknown.
elements.sent-2411-693: Therefore, successful assimilation under these distributional shifts represents both a critical requirement and a significant challenge in the field.
elements.sent-2411-694: For the super-resolution task, observations are generated by applying average pooling to the reference states, followed by the addition of pointwise Gaussian noise.
elements.sent-2411-695: The sparse reconstruction task employs a measurement model combining uniform-stride downsampling with Gaussian perturbation.
elements.sent-2411-696: In the box reconstruction task, measurements within a specified domain are unavailable, while measurements outside this domain are perturbed by Gaussian noise.

(iii) Box reconstruction: The 6th and 7th rows demonstrate the noisy observations with a centering square mask and the corresponding SSLS estimations, respectively.} \label{fig:KolmogorovEvolution}
elements.sent-2411-697: The results demonstrate the exceptional performance of SSLS across all three tasks:.
elements.sent-2411-698: In the super-resolution task, while the observation data lacks most micro-structures present in the reference vorticity field, SSLS successfully reconstructs the majority of these intricate details.
elements.sent-2411-699: The sparse reconstruction results showcase SSLS's remarkable capability to reconstruct the field even when 88.72\% of observation points are masked.
elements.sent-2411-700: This robustness to sparse observations is particularly valuable in applications where measurement acquisition is costly or limited.
elements.sent-2411-701: In the box reconstruction task, SSLS demonstrates impressive performance by accurately reconstructing the field even within the completely masked region where no observations are available.
elements.sent-2411-702: Notably, across aforementioned tasks, the estimated states exhibit significant deviations from the observation data, indicating the substantial influence of prediction information on the assimilation process.
elements.sent-2411-703: The effectiveness of this prediction information will be further examined through ablation studies in subsequent experiments.
elements.sent-2411-704: Furthermore, a mathematical analysis of this efficiency under sparse or partial measurements is presented in Section~\ref{section:experiment:Kolmogorov:ablation .}
elements.subsubsection-2411-006: Ablation study: influence of the prediction score
elements.sent-2411-705: This remarkable performance underscores the fundamental importance of prior information encoded in the prediction score.
elements.sent-2411-706: We now investigate the specific contribution of the prediction score in SSLS through comprehensive comparisons with score-free methods.
elements.figure-2411-023: \includegraphics[width=.9\linewidth]{fig/Kolmogorov_ablation.pdf} \caption{Comparison of results of SSLS and methods without prediction score. From top to bottom: the reference state, observations, estimations of the ensemble MLE, estimations of Langevin sampling without the prediction score, and estimations of SSLS. Here the noise level is set as $\sigma_{\rm obs}=0.3$ and let 90\% points be randomly masked.} \label{fig:KolmogorovLikelihood}
elements.sent-2411-707: To evaluate the impact of the prediction score in SSLS, we employ two comparative methods:.
elements.sent-2411-708: Langevin sampling without prediction score, and.
elements.sent-2411-709: Method (i) employs Langevin sampling where the drift term consists solely of the log-likelihood gradient, with the prediction score component removed.
elements.sent-2411-710: This formulation can be interpreted as a noise-augmented gradient method for maximum likelihood estimation.
elements.sent-2411-711: Method (ii) represents a pure gradient-based MLE approach, obtained by additionally removing the Gaussian noise term from the Langevin sampling formulation.
elements.sent-2411-712: Consequently, although these methods do not explicitly incorporate the prediction score, their state estimates inherently reflect the influence of historical observations through their initialization.
elements.sent-2411-713: As illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:KolmogorovLikelihood , both Langevin sampling without the prediction score (method (i)) and ensemble MLE (method (ii)) produce notably non-smooth state estimations.}
elements.sent-2411-714: This phenomenon has a clear mathematical explanation: the log-likelihood gradient exists only at locations with available measurements and vanishes elsewhere.
elements.sent-2411-715: This gradient absence presents a fundamental challenge in assimilation with sparse or partial measurements.
elements.sent-2411-716: In such scenarios, methods (i) and (ii) can only update in measured locations, while unmeasured locations either experience random perturbations (method (i)) or remain unchanged (method (ii)).
elements.sent-2411-717: This spatially inconsistent updating mechanism leads to discontinuous and non-smooth state estimations in both methods.
elements.sent-2411-718: This score term enables meaningful updates even at unmeasured locations by leveraging the physical constraints and dynamics embedded within it.
elements.sent-2411-719: The score effectively bridges information gaps between measured and unmeasured regions, ensuring appropriate smoothness and physical consistency throughout the domain.
elements.sent-2411-720: This capacity to maintain physical coherence while assimilating sparse measurements allows SSLS to achieve superior reconstruction quality, particularly in regions where observational data is limited or absent.
elements.sent-2411-721: However, point estimation alone proves insufficient, particularly in contexts where estimation reliability is paramount.
elements.sent-2411-722: In such high-stakes scenarios, the quantification of estimation uncertainties becomes critical for informed decision-making~\citep{Sullivan2015Introduction,Adler2025Deep .}
elements.sent-2411-723: A notable advantage of SSLS is its ability to generate multiple ensemble samples from the posterior distribution, enabling the computation of standard deviations that illuminate the quality of the estimated states~\citep{Patel2021GAN,Adler2025Deep .}
elements.sent-2411-724: The estimated pointwise standard deviations are presented in the last row of Figure~\ref{fig:KolmogorovUQ .}
elements.sent-2411-725: This empirical observation aligns with physical intuition.
elements.sent-2411-726: Vorticity, defined as the curl of the velocity field, quantifies the local fluid rotation.
elements.sent-2411-727: Regions of high vorticity indicate intense rotational and swirling motion in the fluid.
elements.sent-2411-728: The uniformly distributed measurement positions prove insufficient to capture high-frequency information in these high-vorticity regions, resulting in greater uncertainty in the estimated states for these areas.
elements.sent-2411-729: One critical measure of uncertainty estimation quality is calibration.
elements.sent-2411-730: A well-calibrated uncertainty estimation ensures that the estimated standard deviation aligns with the pointwise error~\citep{Raad2022Probabilistic,antoran2023uncertainty,Raad2024Conditional .}
elements.sent-2411-731: To analyze this correlation quantitatively, Figure~\ref{fig:KolmogorovBiasVariance plots the standard deviation against the error for pixels down-sampled via max-pooling with a kernel size of 16.}
elements.sent-2411-732: The correlation is examined at three equally spaced time points, revealing a consistent positive correlation between standard deviation and error across all temporal snapshots.
elements.sent-2411-733: This robust uncertainty quantification proves crucial for assessing the reliability of estimated states and offers valuable insights for optimizing observational positions and refining the model.
elements.sent-2411-734: The theoretical analysis establishes SSLS convergence in TV-distance under mild conditions, providing insights into error behavior with respect to hyper-parameters.
elements.sent-2411-735: Extensive numerical experiments demonstrate SSLS's exceptional performance in high-dimensional and nonlinear scenarios, particularly with sparse or partial measurements.
elements.sent-2411-736: Furthermore, SSLS effectively quantifies state estimation uncertainty, enabling error calibration.
elements.sent-2411-737: While the current framework assumes a known state-space model, practical applications often involve uncertain parameters in both dynamics and measurement models.
elements.sent-2411-738: Future work will extend SSLS to enable simultaneous estimation of latent states and model parameters.
elements.sent-2411-739: Additionally, we aim to address the computational burden of score network training at each time step through techniques such as in-context learning.. \bibliographystyle{plainnat .}
